does: This paper presents PromptSleuth, a defense mechanism against prompt injection attacks on large language models that leverages semantic intent invariance as its core principle. The authors construct a comprehensive benchmark that extends existing prompt injection datasets by incorporating paraphrased variations, obfuscated inputs, and multi-task injection scenarios, creating a more challenging evaluation environment. They demonstrate that current defense methods fail under this expanded threat landscape, then propose their approach which detects injection attempts by identifying when the semantic intent of a user's request diverges from the model's intended behavior, achieving improved robustness across diverse attack formulations.
connects: This work builds on the growing body of research addressing adversarial vulnerabilities in LLMs, extending earlier prompt injection benchmarks and defenses that established the basic threat model of instruction-following systems. The paper responds to the limitation that prior defenses overfit to specific injection patterns and paraphrases, continuing a line of work on adversarial robustness in NLP systems. It connects to broader research on intent detection and semantic understanding in conversational AI, drawing on techniques from natural language understanding to distinguish legitimate user intent from malicious injected instructions. The multi-task extension relates to recent work exploring how LLMs handle conflicting directives.
enables: This work opens avenues for developing intent-preserving defenses that can generalize across attack variations, moving beyond pattern-matching approaches to semantic-level robustness. The comprehensive benchmark enables future research to evaluate whether new defense mechanisms truly resist adaptive adversaries rather than specific attack instances. The intent invariance principle could inspire defenses in other vulnerable LLM applications including code generation systems, autonomous agents, and multi-step reasoning tasks where instruction clarity is critical. Future work might explore how to formally specify and verify user intent boundaries, develop stronger attacks against intent-based defenses, and extend the approach to complex multi-party conversation scenarios where intent disambiguation becomes more challenging.
